text: The DaVinci Endoscope Plus provides a magnified HD view of the surgical field during the lysis of adhesions for an incarcerated ventral incisional hernia. The camera captures clear tissue planes between the serosa of the bowel and the abdominal wall. The surgeon uses a forced bipolar instrument in the left hand, which gently grasps the bowel and offers a stronger grasp for handling mesh and suturing, while also providing a safe energy source for bowel operations.

This patient has many adhesions throughout the abdomen and over the liver. The initial presentation was for the incarcerated ventral hernia. The CT scan demonstrated gallstones and the history was consistent with symptomatic cholelithiasis. Given the size of the hernia and the anticipated mesh that would be necessary for an appropriate repair, returning to his abdomen for a subsequent operation would carry considerable morbidity. Therefore, we elected to perform the cholecystectomy during this operation.

The surgeon arranges the ports on the patient's left abdominal wall, lateral to the midclavicular line. After clearing the abdominal wall of adhesions, additional ports are prepared for the cholecystectomy. The small bowel adheres to the liver edge, and adhesions are gently separated using blunt movements and sharp dissection, avoiding cautery near the bowel. The DaVinci Endoscope Plus provides a clear view of the dissection plane, facilitating safe and simple adhesiolysis.

The surgeon begins dissection to expose the medial part of the gallbladder wall during a robotic-assisted multiport cholecystectomy. The assistant uses a laparoscopic retractor to elevate the gallbladder over the liver, which has been mobilized from adhesions. Two additional ports are placed across the midline, including one through the hernia defect, facilitating gallbladder removal and mesh placement.

The surgeon dissects one-third of the way up the gallbladder fossa before applying clips. Three clips are placed on the cystic duct and three on the cystic artery, which are then transected using electrocautery. The gallbladder is removed from the fossa and placed into an endocatch bag, which is advanced through the port placed through the abdominal wall defect. The mesh is also advanced through the same defect, avoiding the need for a larger port or incision.

The surgeon closes the ventral hernia defects using a force bipolar instrument to manipulate and suture with absorbable twelve-inch V-lock sutures. The defects measure approximately fifteen centimeters in length, requiring multiple zero-V lock sutures for closure. The pneumoperitoneum is gradually decreased to eight millimeters mercury to facilitate better approximation of the sutures.

The surgeon uses an 18x22 cm Ovitex biologic mesh with a permanent proline matrix to repair the abdominal wall. Two zero-V-lock sutures are fixed at the far corners of the mesh to secure it in the right lower and upper quadrants. The mesh is lifted to ensure it is centered and covers the defect. A new suture is used to fix the mesh in the left upper quadrant, continuing down the left side of the abdomen.

The surgeon resumes suturing the lower border of the mesh in the patient's right lower quadrant, then moves to the right upper quadrant to continue suturing the upper border. The needle is directed out of the abdominal wall slightly above the mesh border to maintain tension. A new suture line is initiated in the right lower quadrant to complete the right side of the mesh. An additional diagonal suture is placed down the midline of the mesh.
Ideally, this will keep the mesh coapted to the abdominal wall as the body is incorporating the mesh. I hope you found this excessively long video helpful. Thank you for watching.
